question: An increase in temperature usually increases the rate of a chemical reaction because

options.B: more particles have enough energy to react effectively

answer_text: more particles have enough energy to react effectively

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why raising temperature usually speeds up a reaction.
explanation.principle: According to collision theory, particles must collide with enough energy and proper orientation before reaction can occur.
steps: When temperature increases, particles move faster on average.
Faster particles collide more often and with greater energy.
A larger number of collisions then have enough energy to overcome the activation energy.
Therefore the reaction rate increases because more particles have enough energy to react effectively.
explanation.final_answer: B. more particles have enough energy to react effectively
explanation.simple_explanation: Heating makes particles move faster, so more successful collisions happen each second. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Increasing the concentration of a reactant usually increases reaction rate because it

options.A: reduces the number of particles present
options.B: increases the frequency of collision between reactant particles
options.C: stops the reaction from reaching equilibrium

answer_text: increases the frequency of collision between reactant particles

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain how concentration affects reaction rate.
explanation.principle: A higher concentration means more reactant particles are packed into the same volume.
steps: When more particles are present in a given space, collisions happen more frequently.
More collisions per second increase the chance of effective collisions.
That leads to a faster reaction rate.
Therefore increasing concentration increases reaction rate by increasing collision frequency.
explanation.final_answer: B. increases the frequency of collision between reactant particles
explanation.simple_explanation: More particles in the same space means more collisions, so the reaction goes faster. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Factors affecting reaction rate: pressure for gaseous systems
question: For gaseous reactants, increasing the pressure usually increases reaction rate because the gas particles are

options.C: closer together and collide more often

answer_text: closer together and collide more often

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why pressure affects the rate of reaction in gases.
explanation.principle: When gas is compressed, the particles occupy a smaller volume.
steps: With less space available, the particles are closer together.
That makes collisions happen more frequently.
More frequent collisions increase the rate of reaction if other conditions remain the same.
Therefore higher pressure increases rate because the gas particles are closer together and collide more often.
explanation.final_answer: C. closer together and collide more often
explanation.simple_explanation: Compressing a gas packs particles closer together, increasing collision frequency. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Factors affecting reaction rate: surface area
question: Powdered calcium trioxocarbonate(IV) reacts faster with HCl than lumps of the same mass mainly because the powder has

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why a powdered solid reacts faster than large lumps.
explanation.principle: Only the particles at the surface of a solid can collide directly with particles of another reactant.
steps: When a solid is broken into smaller pieces, more surface is exposed.
This exposes more particles to the reacting acid at the same time.
As a result, collisions occur at many more points on the solid.
Therefore the powdered solid reacts faster because it has a greater surface area.
explanation.simple_explanation: More exposed surface means more places where collisions can happen. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A catalyst increases the rate of a reaction by

options.B: providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy
options.C: being used up completely in the reaction

answer_text: providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy

explanation.question_restate: We are to state how a catalyst speeds up a reaction.
explanation.principle: A catalyst does not change the starting materials or final products. It changes the route by which reaction occurs.
steps: The new route has a lower activation energy.
Because the activation energy is lower, more collisions become successful at the same temperature.
This makes the reaction faster.
Therefore a catalyst increases the rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy.
explanation.final_answer: B. providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy
explanation.simple_explanation: Catalysts make it easier for particles to react by lowering the energy barrier. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Light increases the rate of certain reactions mainly because it

options.B: supplies energy needed to start photochemical reactions
options.C: acts as a catalyst in every reaction

answer_text: supplies energy needed to start photochemical reactions

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain how light can affect reaction rate.
explanation.principle: Some reactions are photochemical, meaning they use light energy to initiate or speed up reaction.
steps: Light can provide energy to molecules or ions.
This energy may help break bonds or create reactive species.
As a result, the reaction can proceed faster or begin at all.
Therefore light increases the rate of such reactions because it supplies energy needed for photochemical change.
explanation.final_answer: B. supplies energy needed to start photochemical reactions
explanation.simple_explanation: Some reactions need light energy to create reactive particles or to start the change. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and sodium thiosulphate
question: In the reaction between HCl and sodium thiosulphate, the solution gradually becomes cloudy because

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify why the mixture becomes cloudy in the sodium thiosulphate and hydrochloric acid experiment.
explanation.principle: This reaction produces a fine precipitate of sulfur.
steps: As sulfur particles form, they scatter light passing through the solution.
That makes the mixture look cloudy or milky.
This is why the marked cross under the flask gradually disappears from view in the experiment.
Therefore the cloudiness is caused by sulfur being precipitated.
explanation.simple_explanation: The reaction forms tiny sulfur particles, and these make the solution look cloudy. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and sodium thiosulphate
question: In the sodium thiosulphate and HCl experiment, the time taken for the cross to disappear is used as a measure of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify what the disappearing-cross time tells us in the sodium thiosulphate experiment.
explanation.principle: The faster the sulfur precipitate forms, the more quickly the cross disappears.
steps: If the reaction is fast, the cross disappears in a short time.
If the reaction is slow, the cross takes longer to disappear.
So the time taken gives information about how fast the reaction is proceeding.
Therefore it is used as a measure of reaction rate.
explanation.simple_explanation: Shorter time means faster reaction, so the time is used to compare reaction rates. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: Mg and HCl
question: When magnesium reacts with hydrochloric acid, the gas evolved is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas produced when magnesium reacts with hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: A metal reacting with a dilute acid usually forms a salt and hydrogen gas.
steps: Magnesium reacts with hydrochloric acid to form magnesium chloride and hydrogen.
The balanced idea is Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂.
So the gas observed is hydrogen.
Therefore the correct answer is H₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Magnesium displaces hydrogen from the acid, so hydrogen gas is formed. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and marble
question: The gas evolved when marble chips react with HCl is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas formed when marble chips react with hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Marble chips are mainly calcium trioxocarbonate(IV), CaCO₃.
steps: Acids react with carbonates to give a salt, water and carbon dioxide.
So HCl reacting with CaCO₃ gives CaCl₂, H₂O and CO₂.
The gas produced is carbon dioxide.
Therefore the correct answer is CO₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Acid plus carbonate gives carbon dioxide gas. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: iodine clock reaction
question: In the iodine clock reaction, the sudden colour change occurs because

options.B: iodine accumulates and then forms a blue-black colour with starch

answer_text: iodine accumulates and then forms a blue-black colour with starch

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the sudden colour change in the iodine clock reaction.
explanation.principle: This experiment is designed so that iodine formed is first used up, then later begins to accumulate.
steps: At first, any iodine produced is removed by another reagent in the mixture.
After that reagent is used up, free iodine begins to remain in solution.
When starch is present, iodine forms a blue-black complex with it.
Therefore the sudden colour change occurs because iodine accumulates and reacts with starch.
explanation.final_answer: B. iodine accumulates and then forms a blue-black colour with starch
explanation.simple_explanation: The 'clock' effect happens when free iodine finally appears and turns starch blue-black. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: At the start of a typical reaction-rate curve, the reaction rate is usually highest because

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why the initial rate of a reaction is often the greatest.
explanation.principle: At the beginning of the reaction, the greatest number of reactant particles is present.
steps: More reactant particles mean more frequent collisions.
So the number of effective collisions per second is highest at the start.
As reactants are used up, concentration drops and rate usually decreases.
Therefore the initial rate is highest because reactant concentration is highest.
explanation.simple_explanation: At the beginning there are more reactant particles available to collide, so the reaction is fastest then. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Activation energy is the

options.B: minimum energy particles must have for successful collision

answer_text: minimum energy particles must have for successful collision

explanation.question_restate: We are to define activation energy.
explanation.principle: For a reaction to occur, colliding particles must have enough energy to overcome the energy barrier.
steps: If the particles collide with too little energy, they separate again without reacting.
The minimum energy needed for reaction is called activation energy.
This idea is central to collision theory.
Therefore activation energy is the minimum energy needed for successful collision.
explanation.final_answer: B. minimum energy particles must have for successful collision
explanation.simple_explanation: Activation energy is the minimum energy needed before a collision can lead to reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: On a graph of volume of gas against time, the gradient at any point represents the

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret the meaning of the slope on a gas-volume versus time graph.
explanation.principle: The gradient of any graph shows how quickly the vertical quantity is changing with respect to time.
steps: Here the vertical quantity is the volume of gas produced.
The slope therefore tells how fast gas volume is increasing at that moment.
That is the reaction rate at that point in time.
Therefore the gradient represents the reaction rate.
explanation.final_answer: B. reaction rate at that point
explanation.simple_explanation: Steeper slope means the gas is being produced faster, so the rate is higher. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A catalyst increases reaction rate mainly by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main way a catalyst increases reaction rate.
explanation.principle: Catalysts make reaction easier by reducing the energy barrier that particles must overcome.
steps: With a lower activation energy, more collisions are successful at the same temperature.
So the reaction proceeds faster.
The catalyst itself is not used up overall.
Therefore a catalyst increases reaction rate mainly by lowering activation energy.
explanation.simple_explanation: Lower activation energy means more successful collisions and a faster reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: As a reaction proceeds, its rate often decreases because

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why many reaction rates slow down with time.
explanation.principle: As reactants are used up, fewer reactant particles remain to collide.
steps: Lower concentration means fewer collisions per second.
That reduces the number of effective collisions.
So the rate often falls as the reaction continues.
Therefore the rate decreases because reactant concentration decreases.
explanation.simple_explanation: Fewer reactant particles remain as the reaction goes on, so the reaction usually slows. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: The minimum energy needed for particles to react successfully is called

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall the term for the minimum energy required for successful reaction.
explanation.principle: Not every collision leads to reaction. The particles must collide with enough energy.
steps: That minimum required energy is called activation energy.
If particles do not have this amount, they usually separate unchanged.
This idea is central to collision theory.
Therefore the correct term is activation energy.
explanation.simple_explanation: Activation energy is the minimum energy particles need before reaction can occur. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Heating a reaction mixture usually increases rate because particles

options.C: move faster and collide more energetically

answer_text: move faster and collide more energetically

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the temperature effect on reaction rate.
explanation.principle: Higher temperature increases the average kinetic energy of particles.
steps: As particles move faster, collisions happen more often.
Those collisions are also more energetic.
So more collisions become effective.
Therefore heating usually increases rate because particles move faster and collide more energetically.
explanation.final_answer: C. move faster and collide more energetically
explanation.simple_explanation: Higher temperature makes particles faster and collisions stronger. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

explanation.principle: As reactants are used up, fewer reactant particles remain to collide.
steps: Lower concentration means fewer collisions per second.
That reduces the number of effective collisions.
So the rate often falls as the reaction continues.
Therefore the rate decreases because reactant concentration decreases.
explanation.simple_explanation: Fewer reactant particles remain as the reaction goes on, so the reaction usually slows. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: The minimum energy needed for particles to react successfully is called

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall the term for the minimum energy required for successful reaction.
explanation.principle: Not every collision leads to reaction. The particles must collide with enough energy.
steps: That minimum required energy is called activation energy.
If particles do not have this amount, they usually separate unchanged.
This idea is central to collision theory.
Therefore the correct term is activation energy.
explanation.simple_explanation: Activation energy is the minimum energy particles need before reaction can occur. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Heating a reaction mixture usually increases rate because particles

options.C: move faster and collide more energetically

answer_text: move faster and collide more energetically

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the temperature effect on reaction rate.
explanation.principle: Higher temperature increases the average kinetic energy of particles.
steps: As particles move faster, collisions happen more often.
Those collisions are also more energetic.
So more collisions become effective.
Therefore heating usually increases rate because particles move faster and collide more energetically.
explanation.final_answer: C. move faster and collide more energetically
explanation.simple_explanation: Higher temperature makes particles faster and collisions stronger. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A catalyst increases reaction rate mainly by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main way a catalyst increases reaction rate.
explanation.principle: Catalysts make reaction easier by reducing the energy barrier that particles must overcome.
steps: With a lower activation energy, more collisions are successful at the same temperature.
So the reaction proceeds faster.
The catalyst itself is not used up overall.
Therefore a catalyst increases reaction rate mainly by lowering activation energy.
explanation.simple_explanation: Lower activation energy means more successful collisions and a faster reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Why does a rise in temperature increase the number of effective collisions in many reactions?

options.B: Because more particles have energy greater than or equal to the activation energy
options.D: Because products are removed from the mixture

answer_text: Because more particles have energy greater than or equal to the activation energy

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why higher temperature gives more effective collisions.
explanation.principle: Collision theory says particles must collide with enough energy to react.
steps: As temperature increases, the average kinetic energy of particles rises.
That means a greater fraction of particles has energy equal to or above the activation energy.
So more of the collisions that occur are successful collisions.
Therefore higher temperature increases the number of effective collisions.
explanation.final_answer: B. Because more particles have energy greater than or equal to the activation energy
explanation.simple_explanation: Heating makes more particles energetic enough to react when they collide. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: For a solution reaction, doubling the concentration of a reactant usually makes the reaction faster because

options.A: there are fewer particles in the same volume
options.B: there are more reactant particles per unit volume

answer_text: there are more reactant particles per unit volume

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the effect of concentration on reaction rate.
explanation.principle: A higher concentration means more particles are present in a given space.
steps: With more particles in the same volume, collisions happen more frequently.
More frequent collisions increase the chance of successful collisions.
That is why the reaction generally proceeds faster.
Therefore the correct reason is that there are more reactant particles per unit volume.
explanation.final_answer: B. there are more reactant particles per unit volume
explanation.simple_explanation: More particles in the same space lead to more collisions and a faster reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Factors affecting reaction rate: pressure for gaseous systems
question: For gases, increasing pressure at constant temperature speeds up reaction mainly by increasing

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify what pressure mainly changes in a gaseous reaction system.
explanation.principle: Compressing a gas brings particles closer together.
steps: When gas particles are closer together, they collide more often.
The number of collisions per second therefore rises.
This increase in collision frequency leads to a faster reaction rate.
So pressure mainly speeds up the reaction by increasing collision frequency.
explanation.simple_explanation: Higher pressure packs gas particles closer together so they collide more often. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Factors affecting reaction rate: surface area
question: Which sample of calcium trioxocarbonate(IV) would react fastest with excess HCl?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify which form of marble reacts fastest with acid.
explanation.principle: The rate depends strongly on the surface area exposed to the acid.
steps: Powder exposes the greatest amount of solid surface for the same mass.
That allows acid particles to collide with the carbonate at many more points.
So carbon dioxide is produced more quickly.
Therefore finely powdered marble reacts fastest.
explanation.simple_explanation: Powder has the largest exposed surface area, so it reacts the fastest. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A catalyst does not change the final amount of product at equilibrium because it

options.A: changes only the reaction rate, not the equilibrium position by itself

answer_text: changes only the reaction rate, not the equilibrium position by itself

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why a catalyst affects speed but not the final equilibrium composition by itself.
explanation.principle: A catalyst provides a faster route for both the forward and reverse reactions.
steps: Because both directions are sped up, equilibrium is reached sooner.
However, the equilibrium position itself is not shifted just because a catalyst is added.
So the catalyst changes how fast equilibrium is reached, not the final amount present at equilibrium.
Therefore option A is correct.
explanation.final_answer: A. changes only the reaction rate, not the equilibrium position by itself
explanation.simple_explanation: A catalyst gets the system to equilibrium faster, but does not by itself change the final equilibrium composition. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Which statement best describes the role of light in certain reactions?

options.A: Light always acts as a catalyst
options.B: Light can provide the energy needed to initiate a photochemical reaction
options.D: Light has no effect on reaction rate

answer_text: Light can provide the energy needed to initiate a photochemical reaction

explanation.question_restate: We are to describe how light affects some chemical reactions.
explanation.principle: Some reactions need energy from light in order to begin or proceed faster.
steps: When light is absorbed, it can break bonds or excite particles into more reactive states.
This creates reactive species or starts the reaction pathway.
That is why such reactions are called photochemical reactions.
Therefore light can provide the energy needed to initiate them.
explanation.final_answer: B. Light can provide the energy needed to initiate a photochemical reaction
explanation.simple_explanation: Light can supply the energy some reactions need to get started. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and sodium thiosulphate
question: In the reaction between HCl and Na₂S₂O₃, the disappearance of the cross is faster when the acid is more concentrated because

options.C: collision frequency between reacting particles is higher

answer_text: collision frequency between reacting particles is higher

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why increasing acid concentration makes the disappearing-cross experiment faster.
explanation.principle: Higher concentration means more acid particles are present in the same volume.
steps: With more acid particles present, collisions with thiosulphate particles occur more often.
More frequent collisions lead to faster sulfur formation.
That makes the solution turn cloudy more quickly, so the cross disappears sooner.
Therefore the main reason is increased collision frequency.
explanation.final_answer: C. collision frequency between reacting particles is higher
explanation.simple_explanation: More concentrated acid means more collisions each second, so sulfur forms faster. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: Mg and HCl
question: If equal lengths of magnesium ribbon are placed in excess HCl of different concentrations, the best quantity to compare reaction rate is the

options.B: time taken for the Mg to disappear completely

answer_text: time taken for the Mg to disappear completely

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a suitable practical quantity for comparing rate in the Mg and HCl experiment.
explanation.principle: A good rate comparison uses a measurable change connected directly to the reaction progress.
steps: As magnesium reacts, it gets smaller until it disappears completely.
A faster reaction will consume the same length of Mg in a shorter time.
So the time taken for the Mg to disappear is a useful measure.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. time taken for the Mg to disappear completely
explanation.simple_explanation: If the magnesium disappears sooner, the reaction was faster. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and marble
question: In the reaction between marble chips and HCl, which change would decrease the rate most directly?

options.A: using powdered marble instead of chips

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a change that would directly slow the reaction between marble and acid.
explanation.principle: Dilution lowers the number of acid particles per unit volume.
steps: With fewer acid particles in the same space, collision frequency decreases.
That means fewer successful collisions with the marble surface per second.
As a result, carbon dioxide is produced more slowly.
Therefore diluting the acid decreases the rate.
explanation.simple_explanation: A more dilute acid gives fewer collisions each second, so the reaction slows down. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: iodine clock reaction
question: The time delay before the blue-black colour appears in the iodine clock reaction becomes shorter when the reaction is

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret the meaning of a shorter delay in the iodine clock reaction.
explanation.principle: The delay time is used as a practical indicator of how quickly the reaction is proceeding.
steps: If the reaction is fast, the stage at which free iodine appears is reached sooner.
So the blue-black starch-iodine colour appears after a shorter delay.
A longer delay would indicate a slower reaction.
Therefore a shorter time means the reaction is faster.
explanation.simple_explanation: In the clock reaction, a shorter waiting time means a faster reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: On a graph of concentration of reactant against time, the curve usually becomes less steep as time goes on because

options.A: reactant particles are being used up

answer_text: reactant particles are being used up

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why the concentration-time curve flattens as the reaction continues.
explanation.principle: As reactants are consumed, their concentration falls.
steps: Lower concentration means fewer reactant particles are available for collision.
So the reaction rate decreases.
A lower rate gives a less steep slope on the concentration-time graph.
Therefore the curve becomes less steep because reactant particles are being used up.
explanation.final_answer: A. reactant particles are being used up
explanation.simple_explanation: As reactants run out, the reaction slows and the curve becomes flatter. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Which statement about activation energy is correct?

options.A: It is the same as enthalpy change
options.B: It is lowered by a catalyst
options.D: It is the energy stored in products

answer_text: It is lowered by a catalyst

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the correct statement about activation energy.
explanation.principle: Activation energy is the energy barrier that must be overcome for reaction to occur.
steps: A catalyst provides another pathway with a lower energy barrier.
So the activation energy for the catalyzed route is smaller.
Activation energy is not the same thing as enthalpy change.
Therefore the correct statement is that it is lowered by a catalyst.
explanation.final_answer: B. It is lowered by a catalyst
explanation.simple_explanation: Catalysts help reactions by reducing the activation energy barrier. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Raising temperature usually increases reaction rate because particles

options.B: collide more often and with greater energy

answer_text: collide more often and with greater energy

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why heating speeds up many reactions.
explanation.principle: Higher temperature means particles have more kinetic energy on average.
steps: This makes them move faster.
So they collide more frequently and with greater force.
More collisions now have enough energy to react successfully.
Therefore the reaction rate increases.
explanation.final_answer: B. collide more often and with greater energy
explanation.simple_explanation: Heating makes particles faster and increases the number of successful collisions. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A catalyst is a substance that

options.B: increases rate without being used up overall

answer_text: increases rate without being used up overall

explanation.question_restate: We are to define a catalyst correctly.
explanation.principle: A catalyst speeds up a reaction while remaining chemically unchanged overall at the end.
steps: It works by offering an easier pathway for the reaction.
That makes the reaction proceed faster.
However, the catalyst is not consumed overall by the process.
Therefore a catalyst increases rate without being used up overall.
explanation.final_answer: B. increases rate without being used up overall
explanation.simple_explanation: A catalyst speeds up reaction but is not used up overall. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: The steeper the curve on a product-against-time graph, the

options.D: closer the system is to zero mass

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret slope on a product-time graph.
explanation.principle: A steeper slope means the product amount is increasing more quickly with time.
steps: Rate describes how quickly a quantity changes.
If the graph rises steeply, product is forming rapidly.
That corresponds to a higher reaction rate.
Therefore the steeper the curve, the higher the reaction rate.
explanation.simple_explanation: Steeper graph means faster change, so the reaction rate is higher. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: For a reaction to occur when particles collide, they must have at least the

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the minimum energy condition for successful reaction on collision.
explanation.principle: Colliding particles must overcome an energy barrier before bonds can rearrange.
steps: This barrier is called the activation energy.
If the colliding particles do not have enough energy, no reaction occurs.
So successful collisions need energy at least equal to the activation energy.
Therefore the required energy is the activation energy.
explanation.simple_explanation: Particles must reach the activation energy barrier before they can react. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret slope on a product-time graph.
explanation.principle: A steeper slope means the product amount is increasing more quickly with time.
steps: Rate describes how quickly a quantity changes.
If the graph rises steeply, product is forming rapidly.
That corresponds to a higher reaction rate.
Therefore the steeper the curve, the higher the reaction rate.
explanation.simple_explanation: Steeper graph means faster change, so the reaction rate is higher. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: For a reaction to occur when particles collide, they must have at least the

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the minimum energy condition for successful reaction on collision.
explanation.principle: Colliding particles must overcome an energy barrier before bonds can rearrange.
steps: This barrier is called the activation energy.
If the colliding particles do not have enough energy, no reaction occurs.
So successful collisions need energy at least equal to the activation energy.
Therefore the required energy is the activation energy.
explanation.simple_explanation: Particles must reach the activation energy barrier before they can react. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Raising temperature usually increases reaction rate because particles

options.B: collide more often and with greater energy

answer_text: collide more often and with greater energy

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why heating speeds up many reactions.
explanation.principle: Higher temperature means particles have more kinetic energy on average.
steps: This makes them move faster.
So they collide more frequently and with greater force.
More collisions now have enough energy to react successfully.
Therefore the reaction rate increases.
explanation.final_answer: B. collide more often and with greater energy
explanation.simple_explanation: Heating makes particles faster and increases the number of successful collisions. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A catalyst is a substance that

options.B: increases rate without being used up overall

answer_text: increases rate without being used up overall

explanation.question_restate: We are to define a catalyst correctly.
explanation.principle: A catalyst speeds up a reaction while remaining chemically unchanged overall at the end.
steps: It works by offering an easier pathway for the reaction.
That makes the reaction proceed faster.
However, the catalyst is not consumed overall by the process.
Therefore a catalyst increases rate without being used up overall.
explanation.final_answer: B. increases rate without being used up overall
explanation.simple_explanation: A catalyst speeds up reaction but is not used up overall. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

explanation.principle: Concentration tells us how many particles are present in a given volume.

That increases the number of collisions happening each second.
With more collisions, there are more successful collisions as well.
Therefore the experiment with more concentrated acid is faster because it has more reactant particles per unit volume.
explanation.final_answer: B. more reactant particles per unit volume

question: Which change will most likely increase the rate of a reaction between gases only?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the change that increases the rate of a gaseous reaction.
explanation.principle: For gases, increasing pressure pushes particles closer together.
steps: When gas particles are forced closer together, they collide more often.
More frequent collisions usually give a higher reaction rate.
Cooling would reduce particle energy and generally slow the reaction.
Therefore increasing pressure is the change that most likely increases the rate.
explanation.simple_explanation: Higher pressure means gas particles collide more often, so rate increases. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: If equal masses of zinc granules and zinc powder are separately added to dilute acid, the zinc powder reacts faster because

options.A: it has greater surface area for collision

answer_text: it has greater surface area for collision

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why zinc powder reacts faster than zinc granules.
explanation.principle: For a solid reactant, only the exposed surface can collide with particles in the solution.
steps: Zinc powder exposes much more total surface area than larger granules of the same mass.
This gives more sites where acid particles can collide with zinc.
So more successful collisions occur per second.
Therefore zinc powder reacts faster because it has greater surface area for collision.
explanation.final_answer: A. it has greater surface area for collision

question: Which of the following statements about catalysts is correct?

options.A: A catalyst increases rate by increasing ΔH
options.B: A catalyst lowers the activation energy for both forward and reverse reactions
options.C: A catalyst changes the overall equation permanently
options.D: A catalyst is always a solid

answer_text: A catalyst lowers the activation energy for both forward and reverse reactions

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the correct statement about catalysts.
explanation.principle: A catalyst changes the pathway of reaction, not the starting or final states.
steps: It provides an alternative route with lower activation energy.
Because equilibrium involves both forward and reverse reactions, the catalyst lowers the activation energy for both directions.
It does not change the overall enthalpy or the final equilibrium position by itself.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. A catalyst lowers the activation energy for both forward and reverse reactions
explanation.simple_explanation: Catalysts make both directions easier by reducing the activation energy barrier. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A reaction that is initiated by light is called a

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the name of a reaction started by light.
explanation.principle: Photochemical reactions use light energy to initiate or speed up chemical change.
steps: Light can excite molecules and help them react.
This type of process is described as photochemical.
The name comes from 'photo', meaning light.
Therefore the correct answer is photochemical reaction.
explanation.simple_explanation: If light starts the reaction, it is called a photochemical reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and sodium thiosulphate
question: The reaction between Na₂S₂O₃ and HCl is often studied by observing the

options.B: disappearance of a marked cross beneath the flask

answer_text: disappearance of a marked cross beneath the flask

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall the usual observation in the sodium thiosulphate and hydrochloric acid rate experiment.
explanation.principle: This experiment forms sulfur, which makes the solution cloudy.
steps: The cloudy sulfur suspension gradually blocks the view through the solution.
A black cross placed under the flask becomes harder to see as more sulfur forms.
The time taken for the cross to disappear is used for comparing rates.
Therefore the usual observation is the disappearance of a marked cross.
explanation.final_answer: B. disappearance of a marked cross beneath the flask
explanation.simple_explanation: Sulfur forms and clouds the solution until the cross can no longer be seen. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: Mg and HCl
question: Which factor would most directly speed up the reaction between magnesium ribbon and hydrochloric acid?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a change that directly increases the reaction rate between magnesium and hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: A higher acid concentration means more acid particles are available to hit the magnesium surface.
steps: With more acid particles in the same volume, collision frequency increases.
That increases the number of successful collisions at the metal surface.
So the magnesium reacts more quickly.
Therefore increasing the acid concentration speeds up the reaction.
explanation.simple_explanation: A more concentrated acid gives more collisions with magnesium per second. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and marble
question: In the HCl and marble experiment, if all other conditions are kept constant, the reaction will be fastest when

options.A: the acid is cold and dilute
options.C: powdered marble is used with warm acid

answer_text: powdered marble is used with warm acid

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the set of conditions that would give the highest rate for marble reacting with acid.
explanation.principle: Reaction rate increases with higher temperature, higher concentration and larger surface area.
steps: Powdered marble has a much larger surface area than large pieces.
Warm acid gives particles more kinetic energy and more effective collisions.
Together these conditions strongly increase the rate of carbon dioxide production.
Therefore the reaction is fastest when powdered marble is used with warm acid.
explanation.final_answer: C. powdered marble is used with warm acid
explanation.simple_explanation: Large surface area plus higher temperature gives a much faster reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: iodine clock reaction
question: The iodine clock reaction is useful for comparing rates because the end point is

options.D: the formation of a gas with no colour

explanation.question_restate: We are to state why the iodine clock reaction is a convenient rate experiment.
explanation.principle: A good rate experiment should have a clear, easily timed end point.
steps: In the iodine clock reaction, the solution stays almost colourless for a time.
Then it suddenly turns blue-black when iodine reacts with starch.
This sharp change makes timing easy and accurate.
Therefore the end point is useful because it is a sharp sudden colour change.
explanation.final_answer: B. a sharp sudden colour change
explanation.simple_explanation: The sudden blue-black colour gives a clear moment to stop the timer. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: On a graph of amount of product against time, the curve eventually levels off because

options.B: the reactants are used up or the reaction has effectively finished

answer_text: the reactants are used up or the reaction has effectively finished

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why a product-time graph becomes flat at the end.
explanation.principle: A flat curve means the amount of product is no longer increasing significantly with time.
steps: This happens when the reaction has come to completion or nearly so.
At that stage, one or more reactants have been used up enough that further product formation stops.
So the rate becomes zero or very close to zero.
Therefore the graph levels off because the reactants are used up or the reaction has effectively finished.
explanation.final_answer: B. the reactants are used up or the reaction has effectively finished
explanation.simple_explanation: The curve becomes flat when product stops forming because the reaction is effectively over. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Which change would increase the number of molecules able to overcome activation energy without changing the catalyst?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify what increases the number of molecules with enough energy to cross the activation energy barrier.
explanation.principle: Temperature directly affects the kinetic energy distribution of molecules.
steps: At higher temperature, more molecules have larger kinetic energies.
This increases the fraction with energy at or above the activation energy.
So more successful collisions take place.
Therefore increasing temperature is the correct change.
explanation.simple_explanation: Heating raises particle energy, so more molecules can overcome the activation energy barrier. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: The rate of a reaction at a particular instant can be obtained from the

options.B: gradient of the tangent to the curve at that point

answer_text: gradient of the tangent to the curve at that point

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify how to get instantaneous rate from a reaction-rate graph.
explanation.principle: The instantaneous rate is the rate at one specific moment in time.
steps: On a graph, this is found by drawing a tangent at the chosen point.
The slope or gradient of that tangent gives how fast the quantity is changing at that instant.
That value is the instantaneous rate.
Therefore the correct answer is the gradient of the tangent to the curve at that point.
explanation.final_answer: B. gradient of the tangent to the curve at that point
explanation.simple_explanation: Instantaneous rate comes from the slope of a tangent drawn at the chosen point. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

explanation.principle: When concentration rises, more particles are available in the same volume.
steps: This makes collisions happen more often.
More collisions mean more chances for effective collisions.
So the reaction rate increases.
Therefore concentration speeds up many reactions by increasing collision frequency.
explanation.simple_explanation: Higher concentration means more collisions per second. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A lower activation energy means that

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the effect of lowering activation energy.
explanation.principle: Activation energy is the minimum energy needed for a successful collision.
steps: If that minimum energy is reduced, more colliding particles will have enough energy to react.
So the number of successful collisions increases.
This leads to a faster reaction.
Therefore more collisions will be successful.
explanation.final_answer: B. more collisions will be successful
explanation.simple_explanation: A lower energy barrier means more particles can get over it and react. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: The rate of a reaction at a particular instant can be obtained from the

options.B: gradient of the tangent to the curve at that point

answer_text: gradient of the tangent to the curve at that point

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify how to get instantaneous rate from a reaction-rate graph.
explanation.principle: The instantaneous rate is the rate at one specific moment in time.
steps: On a graph, this is found by drawing a tangent at the chosen point.
The slope or gradient of that tangent gives how fast the quantity is changing at that instant.
That value is the instantaneous rate.
Therefore the correct answer is the gradient of the tangent to the curve at that point.
explanation.final_answer: B. gradient of the tangent to the curve at that point
explanation.simple_explanation: Instantaneous rate comes from the slope of a tangent drawn at the chosen point. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the basic reason temperature affects rate.
explanation.principle: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion of particles.
steps: At higher temperature, the average kinetic energy of particles increases.

So more collisions become successful.
Therefore particles have more kinetic energy.

question: At higher temperature, the reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid is faster mainly because

options.C: more collisions have enough energy to be successful

answer_text: more collisions have enough energy to be successful

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why raising temperature speeds up the magnesium and hydrochloric acid reaction.
explanation.principle: Collision theory says particles must collide with enough energy to overcome activation energy.
steps: When temperature rises, both magnesium-surface collisions and acid-particle collisions become more energetic.
That means a larger fraction of the collisions now has enough energy to react.
So the number of successful collisions per second increases.
Therefore the reaction becomes faster because more collisions have enough energy to be successful.
explanation.final_answer: C. more collisions have enough energy to be successful
explanation.simple_explanation: Hotter particles collide with more energy, so more of the collisions lead to reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: If hydrochloric acid used in the marble-chip experiment is made more concentrated, the reaction becomes faster mainly because

options.A: there are more acid particles in a given volume
options.C: the carbon dioxide formed acts as a catalyst

answer_text: there are more acid particles in a given volume

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why a more concentrated acid speeds up the reaction with marble chips.
explanation.principle: Concentration tells how many particles are present in a certain volume of solution.
steps: If the acid is more concentrated, there are more HCl particles in the same space.
This means the acid can hit the marble surface more often.
So the collision frequency increases and the reaction becomes faster.
Therefore the main reason is that there are more acid particles in a given volume.
explanation.final_answer: A. there are more acid particles in a given volume
explanation.simple_explanation: More concentrated acid means more collisions with the marble surface each second. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Factors affecting reaction rate: pressure for gaseous systems
question: For a gaseous reaction, an increase in pressure at constant temperature is equivalent to

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare the effect of pressure with another common rate factor in gas reactions.
explanation.principle: Compressing gases pushes more particles into the same volume.
steps: This means there are more particles per unit volume.
That is exactly what is meant by higher concentration in a gas system.
So increasing pressure in gases has a similar effect to increasing concentration.
Therefore the correct answer is increase in concentration.
explanation.simple_explanation: Higher gas pressure packs more particles into the same space, just like higher concentration. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Factors affecting reaction rate: surface area
question: Which one of the following changes would increase the rate of reaction between a solid and a solution most directly?

options.A: using larger lumps of the solid
options.B: crushing the solid into smaller pieces
options.C: decreasing the concentration of the solution

answer_text: crushing the solid into smaller pieces

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the change that increases rate by increasing surface area.
explanation.principle: Only particles at the exposed surface of a solid can collide with the solution.
steps: Crushing the solid produces smaller pieces.
Smaller pieces expose more total surface area for the same mass.
That creates more places where collisions can happen.
Therefore crushing the solid into smaller pieces most directly increases the rate.
explanation.final_answer: B. crushing the solid into smaller pieces
explanation.simple_explanation: Smaller pieces expose more surface to the solution, so more collisions happen. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A catalyst can be recovered unchanged at the end of a reaction because it is

options.A: not involved in the reaction at all

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why a catalyst is found unchanged overall at the end of a reaction.
explanation.principle: A catalyst may take part in intermediate steps, but it is formed again before the reaction is complete.
steps: This means the catalyst can participate during the reaction pathway.
However, by the end of the reaction, the same amount is present overall.
That is why it is described as not being used up overall.
Therefore it is consumed and then regenerated overall.
explanation.final_answer: B. consumed and then regenerated overall
explanation.simple_explanation: A catalyst may take part in steps of the reaction, but it is re-formed by the end. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Which of the following best explains why light affects the rate of some reactions?

options.A: It can provide energy that forms reactive particles
options.D: It increases surface area of solids

answer_text: It can provide energy that forms reactive particles

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the role of light in photochemical reactions.
explanation.principle: Light energy can be absorbed by molecules and used to create excited or reactive species.
steps: These reactive species then take part in the reaction more readily.
So light can start or speed up the process.
This is why some reactions proceed only in the presence of light.
Therefore light affects the rate because it can provide energy that forms reactive particles.
explanation.final_answer: A. It can provide energy that forms reactive particles
explanation.simple_explanation: Light can create highly reactive particles, making some reactions faster. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and sodium thiosulphate
question: If the sodium thiosulphate solution is diluted while all other factors remain the same, the time for the cross to disappear will usually

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict what happens to the disappearing-cross time when sodium thiosulphate is diluted.
explanation.principle: Diluting the reactant lowers its concentration and reduces the collision frequency.
steps: With fewer thiosulphate particles per unit volume, sulfur forms more slowly.
So it takes longer for enough sulfur to form to hide the cross.
A longer time means a slower reaction.
Therefore the time for the cross to disappear will increase.
explanation.simple_explanation: Dilution slows sulfur formation, so the cross takes longer to disappear. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: Mg and HCl
question: In the Mg and HCl experiment, if the same mass of Mg is used but the metal is powdered, the reaction would be faster because

options.A: the powdered Mg has greater surface area
options.C: the acid becomes more concentrated automatically

answer_text: the powdered Mg has greater surface area

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why powdered magnesium would react faster than a larger piece of the same mass.
explanation.principle: A larger exposed surface area allows more acid particles to strike the metal at once.
steps: Powdered magnesium exposes many more particles to the acid.
This increases the number of collisions per second at the metal surface.
With more successful collisions, the rate increases.
Therefore the reaction would be faster because powdered magnesium has greater surface area.
explanation.final_answer: A. the powdered Mg has greater surface area
explanation.simple_explanation: More exposed metal surface means more acid collisions and a faster reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and marble
question: Which observation is commonly used to follow the rate of reaction between marble chips and HCl?

options.A: time taken for a fixed volume of CO₂ to be collected
options.D: increase in mass of the marble

answer_text: time taken for a fixed volume of CO₂ to be collected

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a standard way to measure the rate of the marble and hydrochloric acid reaction.
explanation.principle: A rate experiment often follows either the amount of product formed or the amount of reactant used up with time.
steps: In this reaction, carbon dioxide gas is formed.
A common practical method is to measure how long it takes to collect a fixed volume of CO₂.
A shorter time means a faster reaction rate.
Therefore the correct observation is the time taken for a fixed volume of CO₂ to be collected.
explanation.final_answer: A. time taken for a fixed volume of CO₂ to be collected
explanation.simple_explanation: Since carbon dioxide is produced, measuring its volume with time is a good way to compare rates. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: iodine clock reaction
question: In the iodine clock reaction, a shorter clock time means

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret what a shorter clock time means in the iodine clock reaction.
explanation.principle: The clock time is the waiting time before the sudden colour change appears.
steps: If the reaction is faster, it reaches the blue-black colour stage sooner.
That means the waiting time before the colour change is shorter.
A longer waiting time would indicate a slower reaction.
Therefore a shorter clock time means a faster reaction.
explanation.simple_explanation: Less waiting time before the colour change means the reaction is proceeding faster. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A tangent drawn to a rate curve at a particular point is used to determine the

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify what information a tangent gives on a reaction-rate graph.
explanation.principle: A tangent shows the slope of the graph at one exact point.
steps: That slope tells how quickly the measured quantity is changing at that instant.
In kinetics, this is called the instantaneous rate.
It is different from the average rate over a whole interval.
Therefore a tangent is used to determine the instantaneous rate.
explanation.simple_explanation: The slope of the tangent gives the rate at one particular moment. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: If the activation energy of a reaction is lowered, then at the same temperature the reaction rate usually

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict the effect of lowering activation energy while keeping temperature the same.
explanation.principle: A lower activation energy means more particles now have enough energy to react.
steps: At the same temperature, the energy distribution of particles does not change.
But the energy barrier becomes lower.
So a larger fraction of the particles can now react successfully when they collide.
Therefore the reaction rate usually increases.
explanation.simple_explanation: Lowering the energy barrier means more successful collisions, so the rate increases. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: The average rate of reaction over a time interval can be found from the

options.A: change in quantity divided by change in time

answer_text: change in quantity divided by change in time

explanation.question_restate: We are to state how average rate is calculated over a time interval.
explanation.principle: Average rate describes how much a measured quantity changes in a certain period.
steps: You take the change in amount, concentration, mass or volume being followed.
Then divide that change by the time taken.
This gives the average rate over that interval.
Therefore the average rate is change in quantity divided by change in time.
explanation.final_answer: A. change in quantity divided by change in time
explanation.simple_explanation: Average rate tells how much change happened in each unit of time. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A catalyst makes a reaction faster because

options.B: it increases the mass of reactants
options.D: it changes all endothermic reactions to exothermic

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main effect of a catalyst on reaction rate.
explanation.principle: Catalysts provide an easier reaction pathway.
steps: This easier pathway has a lower activation energy.
That means more particles can react successfully at the same temperature.
So the rate of reaction increases.
Therefore a catalyst makes a reaction faster because it lowers activation energy.
explanation.final_answer: A. it lowers the activation energy
explanation.simple_explanation: Catalysts speed up reactions by lowering the energy barrier. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Increasing temperature usually increases reaction rate because

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the basic reason temperature increases reaction rate.
explanation.principle: Higher temperature means particles are moving with more energy on average.
steps: This makes them collide more often and more forcefully.
As a result, more collisions become effective.
That makes the reaction proceed faster.
Therefore increasing temperature usually increases reaction rate because particles have more kinetic energy.
explanation.final_answer: A. particles have more kinetic energy
explanation.simple_explanation: Hotter particles move faster and collide more effectively. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: The energy barrier that must be overcome before reaction occurs is called the

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall the name of the energy barrier that reactants must overcome.
explanation.principle: Reactions do not occur from every collision because many collisions lack enough energy.
steps: The minimum barrier that must be crossed is called activation energy.
If colliding particles do not reach it, they do not react.
This is a key idea in collision theory.
Therefore the energy barrier is called activation energy.
explanation.simple_explanation: Activation energy is the minimum barrier that reacting particles must overcome. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

explanation.principle: Higher temperature means particles are moving with more energy on average.
steps: This makes them collide more often and more forcefully.
As a result, more collisions become effective.
That makes the reaction proceed faster.
Therefore increasing temperature usually increases reaction rate because particles have more kinetic energy.
explanation.final_answer: A. particles have more kinetic energy
explanation.simple_explanation: Hotter particles move faster and collide more effectively. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: The energy barrier that must be overcome before reaction occurs is called the

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall the name of the energy barrier that reactants must overcome.
explanation.principle: Reactions do not occur from every collision because many collisions lack enough energy.
steps: The minimum barrier that must be crossed is called activation energy.
If colliding particles do not reach it, they do not react.
This is a key idea in collision theory.
Therefore the energy barrier is called activation energy.
explanation.simple_explanation: Activation energy is the minimum barrier that reacting particles must overcome. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: The average rate of reaction over a time interval can be found from the

options.A: change in quantity divided by change in time

answer_text: change in quantity divided by change in time

explanation.question_restate: We are to state how average rate is calculated over a time interval.
explanation.principle: Average rate describes how much a measured quantity changes in a certain period.
steps: You take the change in amount, concentration, mass or volume being followed.
Then divide that change by the time taken.
This gives the average rate over that interval.
Therefore the average rate is change in quantity divided by change in time.
explanation.final_answer: A. change in quantity divided by change in time
explanation.simple_explanation: Average rate tells how much change happened in each unit of time. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A catalyst makes a reaction faster because

options.B: it increases the mass of reactants
options.D: it changes all endothermic reactions to exothermic

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main effect of a catalyst on reaction rate.
explanation.principle: Catalysts provide an easier reaction pathway.
steps: This easier pathway has a lower activation energy.
That means more particles can react successfully at the same temperature.
So the rate of reaction increases.
Therefore a catalyst makes a reaction faster because it lowers activation energy.
explanation.final_answer: A. it lowers the activation energy
