question: When the temperature of a reacting mixture is raised, the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution changes so that

options.B: more particles have energies equal to or greater than the activation energy
options.C: all particles have the same energy

answer_text: more particles have energies equal to or greater than the activation energy

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the effect of temperature using the particle-energy distribution idea.
explanation.principle: At higher temperature, the spread of particle energies shifts so that more particles have large kinetic energies.
steps: The activation energy is the minimum energy needed for successful reaction.
When temperature rises, the proportion of particles above this energy becomes larger.
That means more collisions are effective.
Therefore more particles have energies equal to or greater than the activation energy.
explanation.final_answer: B. more particles have energies equal to or greater than the activation energy
explanation.simple_explanation: Heating makes more particles energetic enough to cross the activation-energy barrier. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: If the concentration of hydrochloric acid is halved in a reaction with excess magnesium, the reaction rate will usually

options.B: decrease because fewer acid particles collide with magnesium each second
options.C: remain unchanged because magnesium is in excess

answer_text: decrease because fewer acid particles collide with magnesium each second

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict the effect of halving acid concentration on the reaction rate.
explanation.principle: Lower concentration means fewer reactant particles are present in a given volume.
steps: With fewer acid particles in the same space, the frequency of collision with the magnesium surface decreases.
Because collisions are less frequent, there are fewer successful collisions each second.
So the reaction rate decreases.
Therefore the rate falls because fewer acid particles collide with magnesium each second.
explanation.final_answer: B. decrease because fewer acid particles collide with magnesium each second
explanation.simple_explanation: Diluting the acid means fewer particles hit the magnesium each second, so the reaction slows down. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Factors affecting reaction rate: pressure for gaseous systems
question: Which statement is true for a reaction involving only gases?

options.A: Increasing pressure usually decreases collision frequency
options.B: Increasing pressure usually increases collision frequency
options.C: Pressure has no effect on rate

answer_text: Increasing pressure usually increases collision frequency

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct effect of pressure on gaseous reactions.
explanation.principle: Gas particles occupy space and collide as they move around.
steps: If the pressure is increased, the gas is compressed into a smaller volume.
This brings particles closer together.
As a result, they collide more frequently.
Therefore increasing pressure usually increases collision frequency.
explanation.final_answer: B. Increasing pressure usually increases collision frequency
explanation.simple_explanation: Compressing gases packs particles closer together, so they collide more often. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Factors affecting reaction rate: surface area
question: Which one of the following would give the slowest reaction with excess HCl?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the sample with the smallest effective surface area exposed to the acid.
explanation.principle: Smaller surface area means fewer contact points for collision between the solid and acid particles.
steps: Fine powder has the greatest surface area and reacts fastest.
Small chips also provide more exposed area than one large lump.
A single large lump has the least exposed surface for the same mass.
Therefore one large marble lump gives the slowest reaction.
explanation.simple_explanation: A big lump exposes the least surface area, so it reacts the most slowly. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A catalyst increases the rate constant of a reaction at a given temperature because it

options.B: lowers the activation energy of the reaction pathway

answer_text: lowers the activation energy of the reaction pathway

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why a catalyst changes the rate constant at the same temperature.
explanation.principle: At a fixed temperature, the energy distribution of particles stays the same unless temperature changes.
steps: A catalyst does not change the distribution itself.
Instead, it lowers the activation energy required for reaction.
So a larger fraction of particles can react successfully at that same temperature.
Therefore the rate constant increases because the catalyst lowers the activation energy.
explanation.final_answer: B. lowers the activation energy of the reaction pathway
explanation.simple_explanation: The catalyst makes the energy barrier lower, so more particles can react at the same temperature. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A reaction that is accelerated by sunlight mainly because light breaks some bonds to form reactive species is an example of a

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the type of reaction that uses light to form reactive particles.
explanation.principle: Photochemical reactions depend on light energy to start or speed up chemical change.
steps: Sunlight can provide enough energy to break bonds in some molecules.
This can form reactive radicals or excited species.
Those reactive particles then react more quickly.
Therefore the reaction is a photochemical process.
explanation.simple_explanation: If light starts or speeds the reaction by forming reactive species, the reaction is photochemical. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and sodium thiosulphate
question: The rate of the HCl and sodium thiosulphate reaction is often compared using

options.C: mass of the flask after reaction

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the commonly used rate expression in the disappearing-cross experiment.
explanation.principle: The same visible stage is reached when the cross disappears, so shorter time means faster reaction.
steps: If the time taken is small, the reaction is fast.
If the time taken is large, the reaction is slow.
To make the comparison follow the same direction as rate, rate is often taken as proportional to 1/time.
Therefore 1/time is used for comparing the rate.
explanation.final_answer: B. 1/time for the cross to disappear
explanation.simple_explanation: Fast reaction means short time, so taking 1/time gives a larger value for a faster reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: Mg and HCl
question: If the reaction Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂ is followed by measuring gas volume, the initial steepness of the gas-volume curve represents

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret the first steep part of a gas-volume versus time graph.
explanation.principle: A steeper slope means the gas volume is increasing more quickly.
steps: At the start of the reaction, reactant concentrations are highest.
So hydrogen is produced fastest near the beginning.
The steepness near the beginning therefore represents the initial rate.
Therefore the initial steepness of the curve represents the initial rate of reaction.
explanation.simple_explanation: A steep start to the graph means gas is being formed quickly at the beginning. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and marble
question: When HCl reacts with CaCO₃, the reaction slows down with time mainly because

options.B: the concentration of reactants falls as they are used up
options.D: CO₂ increases the surface area of marble

answer_text: the concentration of reactants falls as they are used up

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why the marble and acid reaction becomes slower as time passes.
explanation.principle: As reaction continues, reactants are consumed.
steps: The acid becomes less concentrated and the available marble surface may also decrease.
With fewer acid particles available, collisions become less frequent.
This reduces the number of effective collisions each second.
Therefore the reaction slows mainly because reactant concentration falls as reactants are used up.
explanation.final_answer: B. the concentration of reactants falls as they are used up
explanation.simple_explanation: As the reactants are used, collisions become less frequent and the reaction slows. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: iodine clock reaction
question: In an iodine clock experiment, if conditions are changed so that the blue-black colour appears sooner, this means

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret a shorter time before the iodine-starch colour appears.
explanation.principle: The clock time tells how quickly the reaction reaches the stage where free iodine builds up.
steps: If the colour appears sooner, the system has reached that point in less time.
That means the underlying reactions are proceeding faster.
So the rate has increased.
Therefore the correct interpretation is that the reaction rate has increased.
explanation.final_answer: A. the reaction rate has increased
explanation.simple_explanation: A shorter clock time means the reaction reached the endpoint faster, so the rate is higher. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: On a concentration-time graph for a reactant, the magnitude of the slope becomes smaller with time because

options.D: the catalyst is changed to a reactant

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why a reactant-concentration curve becomes less steep as time goes on.
explanation.principle: The slope shows how quickly concentration changes.
steps: At first, the concentration is high and collisions are frequent.
As the reactant is consumed, its concentration falls.
This lowers the rate, so the concentration changes more slowly with time.
Therefore the magnitude of the slope becomes smaller because reactants are being consumed.
explanation.simple_explanation: As reactant particles are used up, the reaction slows and the graph becomes less steep. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Which statement best describes the effect of a catalyst on an energy profile diagram?

options.A: It raises both the reactant and product energy levels
options.B: It lowers the peak representing the activation energy barrier
options.D: It removes the products from the diagram

answer_text: It lowers the peak representing the activation energy barrier

explanation.question_restate: We are to describe what a catalyst changes on an energy profile diagram.
explanation.principle: The highest point of the profile corresponds to the activation energy barrier.
steps: A catalyst provides an alternative pathway.
That pathway has a lower activation energy than the uncatalyzed one.
So the peak on the diagram is lower, while the reactant and product levels remain the same.
Therefore the catalyst lowers the peak representing the activation energy barrier.
explanation.final_answer: B. It lowers the peak representing the activation energy barrier
explanation.simple_explanation: Catalysts lower the energy hump that particles must get over. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Increasing temperature usually makes a reaction faster because

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main effect of increasing temperature on reaction rate.
explanation.principle: At higher temperature, particles collide with greater energy.
steps: This means more collisions can overcome activation energy.
So the number of effective collisions rises.
With more successful collisions each second, the reaction becomes faster.
Therefore increasing temperature usually makes a reaction faster because more collisions are effective.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hotter particles make more successful collisions, so the reaction speeds up. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A higher concentration of reactant usually means

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the effect of higher concentration on collision frequency.
explanation.principle: More particles in a given space collide more often.
steps: When concentration rises, reactant particles are closer together on average.
That increases collision frequency.
More frequent collisions generally increase reaction rate.
Therefore a higher concentration usually means more collisions per second.
explanation.simple_explanation: Higher concentration packs particles closer together, leading to more collisions. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A flatter curve on a product-time graph indicates

options.D: a larger mass of product always

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret what a flatter slope means on a product-time graph.
explanation.principle: Slope tells how quickly the product amount is changing with time.
steps: If the curve is flat or less steep, product is forming slowly.
That means the rate of reaction is low at that time.
A steeper curve would mean a faster rate.
Therefore a flatter curve indicates a slower reaction rate.
explanation.simple_explanation: Flat slope means the amount is changing slowly, so the reaction rate is lower. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A successful collision is one in which particles collide with

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the conditions for a successful collision.
explanation.principle: Collision theory requires both enough energy and the right arrangement of particles during collision.
steps: Even if particles collide, reaction may not occur if the energy is too low.
Reaction may also fail if the particles are not oriented suitably.
So both conditions are important.
Therefore a successful collision involves sufficient energy and proper orientation.
explanation.final_answer: B. sufficient energy and proper orientation
explanation.simple_explanation: Particles must hit with enough energy and in the right way before reaction can occur. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.
